primary_legislation.all: Health and Safety (First Aid) Regulations 1981; Health and Safety at Work Act 1974
primary_legislation.northern_ireland: Health and Safety (First Aid) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1982

enforcing_body.england: HSE + CQC ('Safe' domain — AED and emergency response provision reviewed)

notes: Key differences from school: (1) GP practices should have qualified clinical staff on premises during sessions — the first aid needs assessment must distinguish between the statutory first aid at work duty (for staff) and the clinical emergency response capability (for patient emergencies). The latter is a clinical governance matter. (2) AED provision is strongly recommended by NHS/OHID guidance. (3) CQC/HIW/HIS/RQIA assess first aid and emergency response as part of 'Safe'.

steps.description: Locate all first aid kits in the practice. In a GP surgery these are typically: reception/waiting area, staff room, dispensary (if applicable), and any branch site.

Open each kit and check contents against the minimum requirements: BS 8599-1 standard workplace kit contents (or as specified in the first aid needs assessment). Remove any items that are damaged, soiled or past their expiry date.

Note: clinical/medical supplies (suture kits, prescription medicines, resuscitation drugs) are not first aid kit items — they are clinical equipment managed under clinical governance. Do not include them in this kit inspection.

Check the AED (Automated External Defibrillator) if one is installed: confirm the device is operational (status indicator green), pads are in date, spare pads are available, and the device location is clearly signed.
steps.photo_guidance: Photograph the AED status indicator and the first aid kit contents if items have been removed.

steps.description: Restock any items that have been used or have expired. Record: date, kit location, contents checked, items replaced, AED status, inspector name.

Carry out the first aid needs assessment considering: number of employees and their locations; nature of work and hazards (needlestick, COSHH substances, patient aggression, manual handling); opening hours (including early morning, late evening, and any weekend sessions); presence of vulnerable persons (elderly patients, patients with disabilities) in the waiting area.

Determine the minimum number and type of first aiders required: First Aid at Work (FAW, 3-day) or Emergency First Aid at Work (EFAW, 1-day). Ensure adequate cover is available across all session times, including when the clinical lead is absent.
Confirm all designated first aiders hold a current certificate: FAW certificates are valid for 3 years and must be renewed before expiry.
Confirm AED provision is appropriate. NHS/OHID guidance strongly recommends AED installation in GP practices given patient population (cardiac risk). If no AED is present, document the decision.

Confirm first aid provision is consistent with CQC expectations under 'Safe' — CQC inspectors may check first aider certificates, AED maintenance records and emergency equipment location. Consider whether the practice's Major Incident / BLS protocol for in-surgery cardiac arrest is documented (clinical governance, but informed by first aid needs assessment).
Confirm first aid provision meets HSE requirements and HIW standards. HIW inspectors may check first aider and AED records.
Confirm first aid provision meets HSE requirements and HIS standards. HIS quality assessors may check emergency response provision.
Confirm first aid provision meets HSENI requirements and RQIA standards. RQIA may check first aider qualifications and emergency equipment.
Record: date, assessment completed by, first aiders identified, certificate expiry dates, AED provision decision, any gaps identified, actions taken, next review date.
